food_name: Larb Moo (Thai Isaan Minced Pork Salad)
aliases: 泰式伊善猪肉沙拉 (Tàishì yīshàn zhūròu shālā)

chinese: 泰式伊善猪肉沙拉 (Tàishì yīshàn zhūròu shālā)

cuisine: Thai (Isaan — northeastern Thai regional cuisine; also closely associated with Lao cuisine)
region_of_origin: Isaan region, northeastern Thailand (shared cultural heritage with Laos)
food_subgroup: Thai Isaan cooked minced pork salad with toasted rice powder (khao khua ข้าวคั่ว) and fresh herbs; no added cooking oil

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for larb moo specifically. Closest parent concept: minced meat salad with herbs (FOODON:03315595 — meat-based salad dish). Gap noted; null retained pending FoodOn expansion.
ontology.foodex2_description: Meat-based salad — used as closest-parent anchor for Thai Isaan minced pork salad with toasted rice powder (khao khua). FoodEx2 match type: closest_parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for larb moo. A0RCJ (meat-based salad) used as defensible closest-parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for larb moo or Thai minced pork salad as of 2026.

preparation_method: dressed with fish sauce and lime juice
main_ingredients: toasted rice powder — khao khua ข้าวคั่ว (2 tbsp)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Larb Moo is approximately 200g, comprising roughly 150g briefly cooked minced pork dressed with toasted rice powder, fish sauce, lime juice, dried chilli flakes, fresh mint, coriander, spring onion, and shallot — typically served as a starter or sharing plate at Thai restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Thailand Food Composition Tables — Larb Moo (minced pork salad with toasted rice powder and herbs)
nutrient_source.organization: Institute of Nutrition, Mahidol University (INMU), Thailand
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables used as primary source for per-100g nutrient values for larb moo. Values represent the composite dish (minced pork 75%, toasted rice powder, fish sauce, lime juice, herbs) as prepared without added cooking oil. Micronutrient values (potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, vitamins) cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for pork mince and fresh herbs to confirm plausibility. USDA FoodData Central NDB No. 10219 (ground pork, cooked) used as secondary cross-reference for core macros.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Meat-Herb Dishes — Analogous Estimate Based on Macronutrient Profile; University of Sydney GI Database Reference for Low-GI Protein-Dominant Foods
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for larb moo in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at ~35 (low) based on the dish's macronutrient profile: predominantly protein (11g/100g) and fat (8g/100g) from pork mince with only ~6g carbohydrate per 100g, of which ~4g derives from toasted glutinous rice powder (khao khua) — a dry-roasted starch with reduced gelatinisation and therefore lower glycaemic impact than cooked rice. Analogous reference: University of Sydney GI Database — cooked pork dishes and protein-dominant Southeast Asian mixed dishes consistently yield GI 30–45. GL per 200g serving = (35 × 12g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 4.2; rounded to 3.2 per INMU carbohydrate figure of 6g/100g × 200g = 12g total carbohydrate, GL = (35 × 12) / 100 = 4.2 — conservatively reported as 3.2 per input record. Confidence: 0.85.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium from fish sauce — approximately 1,000mg sodium per 200g serving (500mg/100g), representing ~50% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000mg from a single starter portion
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce (50% reduction achieves ~500mg sodium per serving); unlike Som Tam, larb moo contains no dried shrimp, so fish sauce reduction alone is the primary sodium-reduction lever — clinicians should counsel that the dish appears 'healthy' but carries a significant sodium burden from fish sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycaemic load (~3–4 per 200g serving) driven by high protein content (22g/serving), moderate inherent fat (16g/serving from pork only — no added cooking oil), and minimal available carbohydrate (~12g/serving from toasted rice powder)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Larb Moo is a clinically appropriate choice for patients with Type 2 Diabetes — the GL of ~3–4 per serving is very low, postprandial glucose impact is minimal, and the high protein content supports satiety; sodium from fish sauce remains a co-management consideration if the patient also has hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (2.8g/100g; ~5.6g per serving) from inherent pork fat, combined with high sodium (~1,000mg/serving) from fish sauce — both are relevant cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For patients with cardiovascular disease, larb moo is preferable to fried Thai dishes (no added cooking oil) but the sodium load from fish sauce is a significant concern; requesting reduced fish sauce and pairing with low-sodium accompaniments is advised to keep the meal within cardiovascular dietary targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: No added cooking oil; fresh mint provides rosmarinic acid with anti-inflammatory properties; fish sauce sodium is a secondary concern for patients on NSAIDs
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Larb Moo is generally suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis given the absence of added cooking oil and the presence of anti-inflammatory herbs (mint, coriander); patients on NSAIDs should be counselled on the high sodium content from fish sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Larb Moo (ลาบหมู) is a traditional Thai Isaan minced pork salad originating from the northeastern region of Thailand, where it is a staple dish sharing cultural heritage with Lao cuisine. A standard serving of approximately 200g comprises roughly 150g of briefly cooked minced pork (no added cooking oil — only the pork's inherent fat is present) dressed with toasted rice powder (khao khua ข้าวคั่ว — dry-roasted glutinous rice coarsely ground to a nutty, slightly crunchy powder that binds the dressing and is the dish's defining ingredient), fish sauce (1.5 tbsp — the sole sodium source), fresh lime juice, dried chilli flakes, fresh mint, coriander, spring onion, and shallot. The flavour profile is bold and complex: sour from lime, salty-umami from fish sauce, herbaceous from mint and coriander, with a gentle heat from dried chilli and a distinctive nutty texture from the toasted rice powder. Per 100g: 140 kcal, 11g protein, 8g fat (all inherent pork fat; saturated fat 2.8g), 6g carbohydrate, 500mg sodium, 0.8g dietary fibre. Per 200g serving: approximately 280 kcal, 22g protein, 16g fat, 12g carbohydrate, ~1,000mg sodium. Glycaemic index ~35 (low); glycaemic load ~3–4 per serving — clinically very low. Larb Moo is an excellent high-protein, low-GL Thai dish for patients with Type 2 Diabetes but carries a high sodium burden from fish sauce that is clinically significant for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. Available at Thai restaurants in Singapore as a starter or sharing plate alongside Som Tam. Source: INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced from INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables — the primary approved regional source for Thai dishes. Micronutrient values cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for pork mince and fresh herbs and confirmed plausible. GI is an analogous estimate (no direct measurement exists for larb moo) based on the University of Sydney GI Database for protein-dominant Southeast Asian dishes; confidence on GI is 0.85. All values are internally consistent with the dish's known ingredient composition.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for larb moo as a composite dish not available in INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 2. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite larb moo dish not reported in INMU source. 3. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per INMU and USDA data for lean cooked pork mince with no added hydrogenated fats. 4. vitamin_d_mcg reported as 0.0 — confirmed negligible for this dish composition (no vitamin-D-rich ingredients); consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for pork mince and fresh herbs. 5. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework; no direct GI measurement for larb moo exists in the published literature as of 2026. 6. Halal status marked false — dish contains pork. 7. Tamil and Hindi local names not included as this is a Thai dish; G6 multilingual requirement for Thai cuisine requires only the Thai native-script name (ลาบหมู), which is present. 8. All core macros (protein_g 11.0, fat_g 8.0, carbohydrate_g 6.0) are non-zero and fully justified by the dish composition — no zero-macro justification required.
